ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Mushroom Corn Bhaji exists; closest defensible anchor is cooked mushroom food product (FOODON_03311737). Corn and spice components are not captured by this single term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Mushrooms and other fungi - cooked) is the closest defensible match. The dish also contains corn, onion, tomato, and garam masala which are not captured by this single code. Composite dish code not available in FoodEx2 for this specific preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Mushroom Corn Bhaji as a composite Indian vegetable dish. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular codes for regional composite vegetable preparations of this type.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Mushroom Corn Bhaji is approximately 200 g, served as a side dish or main vegetable accompaniment with roti or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (ICMR-NIN, 2017) — composite estimate from mushroom (button), sweet corn, onion, tomato, and oil components; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for button mushrooms (NDB 11260) and sweet corn (NDB 11167)
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for Mushroom Corn Bhaji as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from the weighted average of the primary ingredients: button mushrooms (~40%), sweet corn (~30%), onion (~10%), tomatoes (~10%), oil (~5%), and spices/salt (~5%), using ICMR-NIN 2017 tables and USDA SR Legacy as cross-reference. Energy and macros are consistent with similar North Indian vegetable bhaji preparations.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed vegetable dishes and corn-based preparations — analogous estimate based on sweet corn GI (52, University of Sydney GI Database) and mushroom (GI ~10–15, negligible glycemic contribution), weighted by carbohydrate contribution of each ingredient
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mushroom Corn Bhaji. GI is estimated at 52 (low) based on the dominant glycemic contributor being sweet corn (GI 52, University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Button mushrooms have negligible carbohydrate content and a very low GI (~10–15). The blended dish GI is anchored to sweet corn as the primary carbohydrate source. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 21.6) / 100 ≈ 11.2 for a 200 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt and spice blends
hypertension.guidance: A 200 g restaurant serving provides approximately 420 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should request reduced salt preparation and monitor cumulative sodium intake across the meal, particularly when consumed with sodium-rich accompaniments such as pickles or papadums.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index driven by sweet corn and dietary fibre from mushrooms and vegetables
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 52 and a glycemic load of approximately 11 per 200 g serving, Mushroom Corn Bhaji is a suitable vegetable side dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed as part of a balanced meal; the dietary fibre content (~4.2 g per serving) further moderates postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, cholesterol-free, and rich in potassium and dietary fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mushroom Corn Bhaji is low in saturated fat (~0.8 g per 200 g serving) and contains no cholesterol, making it a heart-friendly vegetable dish; the potassium content (~620 mg per serving) supports vascular health, though sodium intake should be managed in individuals with concurrent hypertension.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mushroom Corn Bhaji is a North Indian vegetable dish consisting of sliced button mushrooms and sweet corn kernels sautéed with onion, tomatoes, and freshly pounded garam masala. The dish is prepared by tempering whole or ground spices in oil, then cooking the onion and tomato base until softened before adding the mushrooms and corn and cooking until tender. The result is a semi-dry, mildly spiced preparation with earthy umami notes from the mushrooms, natural sweetness from the corn, and aromatic warmth from the garam masala blend. In Singapore, this dish is served at North Indian restaurants as a vegetarian main or side accompaniment to roti, naan, or rice. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie, low-fat vegetable dish providing approximately 82 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful dietary fibre (~2.1 g/100 g), potassium (~310 mg/100 g), and B-vitamins from the mushroom component. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Clinically, it is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease risk, and those following calorie-controlled or plant-forward diets. Sodium content from restaurant preparation warrants attention for hypertensive individuals.
common_user_queries: Is mushroom corn bhaji good for diabetics?
What spices are in mushroom corn bhaji?
Is mushroom corn bhaji gluten free?
meal_context: side dish with roti or naan

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Mushroom Corn Bhaji as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN 2017 component data for button mushrooms, sweet corn, onion, tomato, and oil, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate anchored to sweet corn (University of Sydney GI Database). Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is defensible and ingredient proportions are consistent with the dish description, but no direct laboratory analysis of this specific restaurant preparation has been performed.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-weighted estimates based on ICMR-NIN 2017 and USDA SR Legacy; no direct analysis of this dish exists in approved sources. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA SR Legacy for this ingredient combination. 3. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN 2017 for the primary ingredients of this dish. 4. Trans fat is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as the dish uses vegetable oil and plant-based ingredients with no industrial trans fat sources. 5. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero by ICMR-NIN component data. 6. Cholesterol is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish; confirmed zero. 7. GI is an analogous estimate (52, low) anchored to sweet corn as the primary carbohydrate contributor per University of Sydney GI Database. 8. Sodium estimate of 210 mg/100 g reflects typical restaurant-level salting; home-prepared versions may be lower. 9. Serving size of 200 g is an estimate for a typical restaurant portion; actual portion weights may vary by restaurant.
